'''Glenn Adam Morrison''' (born 28 May 1976), is an Australian former professional [[rugby league]] footballer and coach. He played for the [[Parramatta Eels]], the [[Balmain Tigers]], the [[North Sydney Bears]] and the [[North Queensland Cowboys]] in the [[National Rugby League]] before moving to England to play for the [[Bradford Bulls]] and then the [[Wakefield Trinity|Wakefield Trinity Wildcats]] ([[Captain (sports)|captain]]). He was later the head coach of the [[Dewsbury Rams]]. In 2021, Morrison was appointed Head coach of [[Cleveland Rugby League]], in the newly created [[North American Rugby League]].

==Playing career==
===Early career===
Morrison began playing junior football for the Terrigal Sharks in the [[Central Coast Rugby League]].{{citation needed|date=March 2017}}

===Balmain Tigers===
Morrison started his career with Balmain in 1996, where he played 21 times in his début season, scoring 4 tries. He went on to play another 20 times in 1997, scoring 3 tries, before he left Balmain for the [[North Sydney Bears]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2019/12/21/december-21-gasniers-last-game-crichton-gets-the-chop/|title=December 21: Gasnier's last game; Crichton gets the chop|publisher=National Rugby League|date=20 December 2020|access-date=27 December 2019|archive-date=27 December 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191227153801/https://www.nrl.com/news/2019/12/21/december-21-gasniers-last-game-crichton-gets-the-chop/|url-status=live}}</ref>

===North Sydney Bears===
Morrison signed a two-year deal with the North Sydney Bears in 1998 where he played 42 times scoring 9 tries. He had his most successful season in 1999 when he scored 24 points in 20 games. Morrison was a member of The North Sydney side when they played their final ever game in first grade against the [[North Queensland Cowboys]] in [[Townsville]] ending 91 years of playing in the top grade.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/seasons/nrl-1999/round-26/north-queensland-vs-north-sydney/summary.html|title=NRL 1999 – Round 26 – Rugby League Project|access-date=2 August 2017|archive-date=2 August 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170802045727/http://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/seasons/nrl-1999/round-26/north-queensland-vs-north-sydney/summary.html|url-status=dead}}</ref>

===North Queensland Cowboys===
Morrison was on the move again when he signed for North Queensland Cowboys in 2000. In 2001, Morrison was named the Cowboys' player of the year when he scored 13 tries is 23 games.<ref>[http://www.cowboys.com.au/default.aspx?s=honours Honours]  {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111012233017/http://www.cowboys.com.au/default.aspx?s=honours |date=12 October 2011 }} at ''cowboys.com.au''</ref> He had his most successful spell at the Cowboys scoring 168 points in 94 games. He scored 60 points in 2002 playing only 15 times.

Morrison was selected for [[City vs Country Origin|Country Origin]] in 2001, 2003, 2004, and 2005.

===Parramatta Eels===
Morrison left the Cowboys in 2004 to join Parramatta in 2005 where he spent 2 seasons scoring 60 points in 45 games.  In 2005, Morrison was part of the Parramatta side which won the minor premiership but lost 29–0 in the preliminary final against North Queensland. 2006 was his final season in the NRL before he left for the [[Super League]].<ref>{{Cite web|url = https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/teams/eels/nathan-hindmarsh-on-parramattas-last-table-topping-team-where-his-former-teammates-are-now/news-story/fea0ebc7740f2dbbf8884bae9db77210|title = Nathan Hindmarsh on Parramatta's last table topping team, where his former teammates are now|date = 13 March 2017|access-date = 26 July 2018|archive-date = 5 June 2019|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20190605145142/https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/teams/eels/nathan-hindmarsh-on-parramattas-last-table-topping-team-where-his-former-teammates-are-now/news-story/fea0ebc7740f2dbbf8884bae9db77210|url-status = live}}</ref>

===Bradford Bulls===
In January 2007, Morrison signed a three-year deal with the [[Bradford Bulls]], where head coach [[Steve McNamara]] appointed Morrison as vice-captain. That same year, Morrison made the [[Super League]] Dream Team. Morrison also won the T&A Player of the Year as well as the coaches' award, Players' Player of the Year and Supporters' Player of the Year titles in 2007.{{citation needed|date=March 2017}} In 2008, Morrison made 733 tackles, the sixth highest in the entire competition.{{citation needed|date=March 2017}}

===Wakefield Trinity===
After three years with the Bradford Bulls, Morrison signed with the [[Wakefield Trinity|Wakefield Trinity Wildcats]] for the 2010 season. After a successful first season at Wakefield, he signed for a further season in 2011.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.wakefieldwildcats.co.uk/news/glenn-morrison/2009/10/|title=News – Glenn Morrison|author=Wakefield Wildcats|work=wakefieldwildcats.co.uk|access-date=17 July 2015|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131019171930/http://www.wakefieldwildcats.co.uk/news/glenn-morrison/2009/10/|archive-date=19 October 2013}}</ref>

In June 2011, Morrison was selected as a reserve in the inaugural [[International Origin|England vs Exiles Origin match]], at Headingley. He was promoted to the interchange bench after Warrington centre Matt King dropped out for personal reasons, and played for a large part of the game. Despite missing out on the Man of the Match trophy (picked up by Castleford Tigers stand off Rangi Chase), Morrison's performance was picked out as being amongst the best on the night.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.wakefieldwildcats.co.uk/news/wildcats-captain-included-in-exiles-squad/2011/04/|title=News – Wildcats Captain Included in Exiles Squad|author=Wakefield Wildcats|work=wakefieldwildcats.co.uk|access-date=17 July 2015|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130505092611/http://www.wakefieldwildcats.co.uk/news/wildcats-captain-included-in-exiles-squad/2011/04/|archive-date=5 May 2013}}</ref>

On 11 November 2011, Morrison announced his immediate retirement from the game, acting on advice from health professionals, due to a serious shoulder injury. He remained at the Wakefield Trinity Wildcats, in the capacity of assistant coach.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2011/nov/11/wakefield-glenn-morrison-injury|title=Wakefield's Glenn Morrison forced by shoulder injury to retire|date=11 November 2011|work=[[The Guardian]]|access-date=13 November 2011}}</ref> He played 46 times for Wakefield, scoring 10 tries during his two-year spell at the club.

===Bradford Salem===
Glenn Morrison crossed codes for a 2019 charity game in the Richard Birkett memorial game playing on behalf of Bradford Salem.

Following his charity run out; Glenn signed for Salem to play through the 2019 winter season, debuting at fly half in the opening preseason game against Yarnbury XV.

==Coaching career==
On 5 September 2012, it was announced Morrison was leaving Wakefield to take up the position of head coach for two years at the [[Dewsbury Rams]].<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.code13rugbyleague.com/2012/09/05/glenn-morrison-named-new-dewsbury-coach/ |title=Code13 Rugby League news | Glenn Morrison named new Dewsbury coach |access-date=2012-09-06 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121126215316/http://www.code13rugbyleague.com/2012/09/05/glenn-morrison-named-new-dewsbury-coach/ |archive-date=26 November 2012 }}</ref>

During the 2014 pre-season Morrison was reported by ''[[The New Zealand Herald]]'' as being short-listed for the Kiwis coaching job.<ref>{{cite news|last=Brown|first=Michael|title=Four shortlisted for Kiwis job|url=http://www.nzherald.co.nz/league/news/article.cfm?c_id=79&objectid=11197043|access-date=5 February 2014|newspaper=[[The New Zealand Herald]]|date=5 February 2014|archive-date=5 February 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140205074041/http://www.nzherald.co.nz/league/news/article.cfm?c_id=79&objectid=11197043|url-status=live}}</ref>

After a run of defeats during the early stages of the 2017 championship season, Morrison announced he would be stepping down as head coach at Dewsbury Rams on 27 March after a near five-year tenure .<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.dewsburyrams.co.uk/news/article/52203/rams-announce-morrison-departure |title=Rams announce Morrison departure |website=Dewsburyrams.co.uk |access-date=2017-03-30 |archive-date=30 March 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170330175211/http://www.dewsburyrams.co.uk/news/article/52203/rams-announce-morrison-departure |url-status=live }}</ref> As of 2018, Morrison is a rugby coach at [[Bradford Grammar School]].

Glenn coached the [[Jamaica national rugby league team|Jamaica]] national team for one fixture in the 34–12 defeat to [[France national rugby league team|France]] in Perpignan on 13 Oct 2017.

On 19 April 2021 it was announced that he had been appointed as Director of Rugby at [[Cleveland Rugby League]] in the new [[North American Rugby League]].<ref>{{cite web|website=Love Rugby League|date=19 April 2021|accessdate=20 April 2021|title=Glenn Morrison becomes director of rugby at Cleveland|url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/glenn-morrison-becomes-director-of-rugby-at-cleveland/|archive-date=19 April 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210419165633/https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/glenn-morrison-becomes-director-of-rugby-at-cleveland/|url-status=live}}</ref>
